proofs.proof_tex:
Proof. Fix m >= 2 and a fixed admissible family C in Mcal_m(G,u,v,I_v,Pi_v). Let J be the finite list of all distinct ordered cumulant arguments that occur in the entries kappa_X(i_r,p_s,C_r), r,s=1,...,q, and kappa_X(i_r,v,C_r), r=1,...,q. Write T in R^d for the corresponding population cumulant vector and T_hat_n for the vector of the matching empirical k-statistics. The entries of K_X(I_v,C) and k_X(v;I_v,C) are coordinate projections of T, and the entries of Khat_n(I_v,C) and khat_n(v;I_v,C) are the same coordinate projections of T_hat_n.

Define the rational map g on cumulant vectors by filling K(t) and k(t) with the coordinates of t and setting
\[
g(t)=\frac{\det(K(t)\text{ with column }s_u\text{ replaced by }k(t))}{\det K(t)}.
\]
By def:plugin-estimator, lambdahat_uv,n(C)=g(T_hat_n) on the event det Khat_n(I_v,C) != 0. By ass:denominator-margin, |det K(T)|=|det K_X(I_v,C)| >= c0 > 0. Since t -> det K(t) is continuous, there is an open neighborhood U of T on which det K(t) is bounded away from zero; hence g is continuously differentiable on U.

By lem:sample-cumulant-clt-delta and ass:finite-moments, T_hat_n ->p T and sqrt(n)(T_hat_n-T) ->d N(0,Sigma_T), where Sigma_T is the covariance matrix of the limiting finite-dimensional sample-cumulant vector. In particular P(T_hat_n in U) -> 1, so P(det Khat_n(I_v,C) != 0) -> 1 and the possible behavior of the ratio outside U is asymptotically irrelevant. The continuous mapping theorem gives g(T_hat_n) ->p g(T). By thm:closed-form-coefficient, g(T)=lambda_uv. Therefore lambdahat_uv,n(C) ->p lambda_uv.

For the distributional limit, Taylor expand g at T on U:
\[
g(T_hat_n)-g(T)=\nabla g(T)^T(T_hat_n-T)+r_n,
\]
where r_n/||T_hat_n-T|| ->p 0 because g is continuously differentiable at T. Since T_hat_n-T=O_p(n^{-1/2}), sqrt(n)r_n ->p 0. Combining this expansion with the finite-dimensional normal limit for T_hat_n gives
\[
\sqrt n\{lambdahat_uv,n(C)-lambda_uv\}\Rightarrow N\left(0,\nabla g(T)^T\Sigma_T\nabla g(T)\right).
\]
This is a mean-zero normal law, possibly degenerate, and its variance is exactly the multivariate delta-method variance obtained by applying the gradient of the determinant-ratio map to the vector of sample cumulants entering Khat_n(I_v,C) and khat_n(v;I_v,C).

added_lemmas.statement: Fix a finite list of joint cumulants of coordinates of X, each of order at most m, and let T be the population cumulant vector and T_hat_n the corresponding vector of empirical k-statistics computed from n i.i.d. observations of X. Under ass:finite-moments, T_hat_n converges in probability to T and sqrt(n)(T_hat_n-T) converges in distribution to a mean-zero multivariate normal vector. Moreover, if g is continuously differentiable in a neighborhood of T, then sqrt(n)(g(T_hat_n)-g(T)) converges in distribution to the mean-zero normal law with variance Dg(T) Sigma_T Dg(T)^T, where Sigma_T is the covariance matrix of the limiting sample-cumulant vector.
added_lemmas.proof_tex:
Proof. Let the finitely many cumulant arguments be J_1,...,J_d, with |J_l| <= m. For every block B appearing in a partition of any J_l, introduce the raw moment mu_B=E[prod_{a in B} X_a]. Only finitely many such raw moments are needed, and each has total degree at most m. Let mu be the resulting finite raw-moment vector and let mu_hat_n be its empirical analogue formed from the n i.i.d. observations of X.

The joint cumulant of an ordered tuple J is the finite partition polynomial
\[
\kappa_X(J)=\sum_{\pi\in\Pi(J)} (|\pi|-1)!(-1)^{|\pi|-1}\prod_{B\in\pi} E\left[\prod_{a\in B}X_a\right].
\]
Thus the population cumulant vector is F(mu) for a polynomial map F. Ass:finite-moments states that all joint moments of X up to order 2m are finite. Hence every monomial entering mu has a finite second moment, because its square has total degree at most 2m. The ordinary finite-dimensional law of large numbers and central limit theorem for the empirical average vector mu_hat_n therefore give
\[
mu_hat_n ->p mu, \qquad \sqrt n(mu_hat_n-mu) ->d N(0,Omega)
\]
for the finite covariance matrix Omega of the raw-moment monomial vector.

The plug-in cumulant vector F(mu_hat_n) is consequently consistent, and the first-order Taylor expansion of the polynomial F gives
\[
\sqrt n\{F(mu_hat_n)-F(mu)\} ->d N(0, DF(mu)Omega DF(mu)^T).
\]
It remains only to pass from plug-in cumulants to the empirical k-statistics named in the frozen core. For fixed cumulant order, each k-statistic is a finite-sample unbiased polynomial in the empirical power sums whose coefficients are rational functions of n and whose coefficientwise limit is the corresponding plug-in cumulant polynomial. Therefore, for the fixed finite list J_1,...,J_d, the difference between the k-statistic vector T_hat_n and F(mu_hat_n) is O_p(n^{-1}); the empirical raw moments appearing in those polynomials are O_p(1) by the preceding law of large numbers. Hence sqrt(n){T_hat_n-F(mu_hat_n)} ->p 0. Slutsky's theorem gives T_hat_n ->p T and
\[
\sqrt n(T_hat_n-T) ->d N(0,Sigma_T),\qquad Sigma_T=DF(mu)Omega DF(mu)^T.
\]
Finally, if g is continuously differentiable in a neighborhood of T, Taylor's formula gives g(T_hat_n)-g(T)=Dg(T)(T_hat_n-T)+o_p(||T_hat_n-T||). Since T_hat_n-T=O_p(n^{-1/2}), multiplying by sqrt(n) and applying Slutsky's theorem yields the stated delta-method normal limit with variance Dg(T) Sigma_T Dg(T)^T.
